title: ci: redeploy consumers on package changes
author: ElliotEtag
pr_body_excerpt:
- Adds `packages/sdk/**` and `packages/ui/**` to enter and website production deploy path filters.
- Publishes SDK/UI prereleases under a version-derived npm dist-tag (`alpha` for prereleases, `latest` for stable versions); MCP/CLI publishing is unchanged.
- Updates automatic app deploys to deploy every changed registered app instead of only the first changed app.

Scope note:
- This PR intentionally does not add package README stability warnings. Those should ship in the SDK/UI package PRs together with the alpha version bumps, so npm users see the warning on the exact alpha package they install.

Validation:
- `git diff --cached --check`
- Ruby YAML parse for all four edited workflow files
- `gh auth status`

Note: `actionlint` is not installed locally, so workflow linting was limited to YAML parse and shell/diff review.

gist.headline: Smarter pipelines
gist.blurb: Our CI pipelines just got a brain upgrade. Now, changes to the SDK or UI automatically trigger deployments for the apps that depend on them, and we're properly tagging alpha releases on npm.
gist.summary: Updated GitHub Actions workflows to trigger enter and website deployments on SDK/UI package changes, publish prereleases with an alpha npm tag, and deploy all changed apps simultaneously instead of just the first one.
gist.impact: Internal deployments are now more reliable and automated, ensuring that downstream consumers always use the latest package versions without manual intervention.
gist.visual_concept: A robot managing a conveyor belt or pipeline of boxes (packages/apps), with Polly the bee overseeing the smooth operation, representing automated CI/CD pipelines.
gist.image_prompt: Cozy pixel art scene of a chunky robot with a CRT monitor head managing a glowing lime green (#ecf874) conveyor belt carrying small pixelated boxes. The cute bee mascot hovers nearby holding a tiny clipboard, checking off successful deployments. Soft pastel gradient background, warm lighting, lo-fi 8-bit aesthetic like Stardew Valley. Emotionally warm, nostalgic but beautiful.
